title: Lighting a bee smoker
subtitle: The fuel, the lighting order, and the bellows rhythm that keeps a smoker burning for a full inspection.
excerpt: A smoker that goes out halfway through a hive inspection turns a routine visit into a difficult one. The fix is almost always the same: wrong fuel, too little of it, or bellows left idle for too long. This tutorial walks the lighting sequence and the maintenance rhythm for a cold smoker from scratch.
sourceNotes:
National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024 edition), hive-tool and smoker sections. https://www.nationalbeeunit.com
Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), Chapter III on smoker management, Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: A stainless-steel canister with a bellows that burns smouldering fuel to produce cool smoke. Directed at the hive entrance and under the crown board before opening, it interrupts the colony's alarm response.

content.text: is the one piece of beekeeping kit that cannot be improvised at the last minute. If it goes cold ten minutes into a hive inspection, the colony notices and the inspection becomes harder. Light it before you suit up, at least five minutes before you approach the hive, so you have time to confirm it is burning and holding heat.

Untreated hessian sacking is the most reliable fuel for a beginner: it catches easily, burns slowly, and the smoke stays cool. Dried pine needles, hay, and cardboard all work. Compressed wood-fibre smoker pellets are clean and consistent. Never use any material that has been creosoted, painted, or chemically treated, and avoid scented fire-lighters, which produce volatile compounds the bees react to badly.

Open the smoker lid. Light a small handful of fire-starter material, dry grass or a corner of cardboard, and drop it into the canister. Work the bellows steadily until it is burning properly. Now pack in your main fuel, hessian torn into palm-sized pieces, and push it down loosely with a hive tool or a stick. Loose packing is important: too tight and the smoulder chokes out, too loose and it burns too hot and goes through the fuel in twenty minutes.

Close the lid and pump the bellows six to eight times. Check the spout: you want a steady flow of cool, white smoke, not heat and sparks. Cool smoke is what you want at the entrance. A hot, scorching flow stings bees rather than calming them.
Keeping it burning through an inspection

A loaded smoker will smoulder for thirty to sixty minutes if you give the bellows three or four slow pumps every two to three minutes. If you let it sit idle, the fuel cools and the fire goes out. Keep one hand on the bellows while the other hand works frames. You do not need a constant stream of smoke: a few puffs at the entrance before you open the box, a couple under the crown board, and then as needed if the bees cluster on the top bars or start flying in numbers.
At the end of the inspection

Put the smoker down on bare soil or stone, never on grass, which can catch. Push a cork or a wad of fresh green grass into the spout to starve the smoulder of air. Leave it for ten minutes, then open and check there is no remaining heat before putting it away. A smoker left burning in a bag or toolbox is a fire risk.

entries.cause: Fuel packed too tightly, or bellows not worked regularly
entries.fix: Pack more loosely next time. If it goes cold mid-visit, close the hive, take the smoker away from the bees, and relight with a fire-starter before going back in.

entries.symptom: Smoke is hot and thin rather than cool and thick
entries.cause: Too little fuel or wrong fuel
entries.fix: Add more hessian or pellets on top. Cool smoke is produced by smouldering, not by full combustion.

entries.symptom: Bees are not calming despite smoke
entries.cause: Smoke too hot, wrong fuel, or colony very agitated from a previous disturbance
entries.fix: Check fuel. If the colony is already wound up, close up and return another day rather than persisting.
